questions.question: A hard disk is connected to a 500 MHz processor through a DMA controller. Assume that the initial set-up of a DMA transfer takes 1000 clock cycles for the processor, and assume that the handling of the interrupt at DMA completion requires 500 clock cycles for the processor. The hard disk has a transfer rate of 2000 Kbytes/sec and average block transferred is 4 Kbytes. What fraction of the processor time is consumed by the disk, if the disk is actively transferring 100% of the time?
questions.to_find: Fraction of processor time consumed by the disk during active DMA transfer

understand.plain: A disk uses DMA to transfer data, so the CPU is only involved at the start (setup) and end (interrupt) of each block transfer. We calculate how many block transfers happen per second, multiply by the CPU cycles consumed per transfer, and divide by the total CPU cycles per second to get the fraction of CPU time used.

keywords.explain: A mechanism that allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention for each byte, freeing the CPU except for initial setup and final interrupt.
keywords.example: A disk controller uses DMA to copy an entire 4 KB block into RAM while the CPU continues executing other instructions.

keywords.explain: The speed at which data moves from the I/O device to memory, measured in bytes per second.
keywords.example: 2000 KB/s means 2,000,000 bytes are transferred every second.

keywords.explain: The basic unit of processor time; number of operations a processor performs per second equals its clock frequency.
keywords.example: A 500 MHz processor executes 500,000,000 clock cycles per second.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing CPU activity: short burst of 1000 cycles for DMA setup, long idle period while DMA transfers 4 KB, then short burst of 500 cycles for interrupt handling. The idle period dominates.

given.aim: Calculate the fraction of processor time used by the disk when it is transferring 100% of the time.

terms.example: In 1 second, CPU executes 5 x 10^8 cycles total.
terms.connects: This is the denominator for computing the fraction of CPU time.

terms.term: DMA setup = 1000 cycles, interrupt handling = 500 cycles
terms.meaning: Each block transfer requires the CPU for 1000 + 500 = 1500 clock cycles total.
terms.example: Per 4 KB block, CPU spends 1500 cycles; the rest of the transfer time it is free.
terms.connects: CPU cycles consumed per DMA block transfer.

terms.term: Transfer rate = 2000 KB/s, block size = 4 KB
terms.meaning: Number of blocks transferred per second = 2000 KB/s / 4 KB = 500 blocks/second.
terms.example: In one second, 500 DMA block transfers complete.
terms.connects: This gives the rate at which the CPU is interrupted.

given.plan: Compute blocks per second = transfer_rate / block_size. CPU cycles consumed per second = blocks_per_second x cycles_per_block. Fraction = CPU cycles consumed / total CPU cycles per second.

steps.title: Compute number of DMA blocks transferred per second
steps.note: The disk is actively transferring 100% of the time, so this full rate applies continuously.

steps.title: Compute CPU cycles consumed per second by DMA
steps.note: The CPU only runs during DMA setup and interrupt handling — not during the actual data transfer.

steps.title: Compute fraction of processor time
steps.note: This means the processor spends only about 0.15% of its time servicing disk DMA — demonstrating why DMA is efficient for bulk transfers.

solution.result: The fraction of processor time consumed by the disk is 0.0015 (i.e., 0.15% of processor time).

questions.formula_note: DMA overhead fraction = (blocks/sec x CPU_cycles_per_block) / processor_clock_speed. DMA frees the CPU during data transfer; CPU is only involved at setup and interrupt.

questions.question:
The correct matching for the following pairs is:
(A) DMA I/O         (1) High speed RAM
(B) Cache           (2) Disk
(C) Interrupt I/O   (3) Printer
(D) Condition Code Register  (4) ALU

A. A-4, B-3, C-1, D-2
B. A-2, D-1, C-3, D-4
C. A-4, B-3, C-4, D-1
D. A-2, B-1, C-3, D-4
questions.to_find: Correct matching of I/O mechanisms and components to their associated hardware/use-cases

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of which I/O technique or component is most closely associated with a particular hardware device or unit. DMA is used for bulk data devices like disks; Cache is high-speed RAM between CPU and main memory; Interrupt I/O is used for slow character devices like printers; Condition Code Register stores flags set by the ALU.

keywords.explain: Direct Memory Access: data is transferred in bulk between memory and I/O device without CPU involvement for each byte.
keywords.example: Hard disk uses DMA to transfer entire sectors to RAM.

keywords.explain: A register that holds status flags (zero, carry, overflow, sign) set as a side-effect of ALU operations.
keywords.example: After ADD R1, R2, the zero flag is set if the result is 0.

understand.visual_alt: Table matching four I/O concepts: DMA with Disk, Cache with High-speed RAM, Interrupt I/O with Printer, and Condition Code Register with ALU.

given.aim: Match each I/O method or component to the hardware it is most associated with.

terms.meaning: DMA is ideal for bulk transfer devices like hard disks where large blocks of data move at high speed.
terms.example: Disk DMA transfers a 4 KB sector directly into RAM.
terms.connects: Eliminates CPU involvement for each byte during bulk disk I/O.

terms.meaning: Cache is implemented using high-speed SRAM placed between the CPU and slower main (DRAM) memory.
terms.example: L1 cache is typically 32 KB of fast SRAM.
terms.connects: Cache exploits temporal/spatial locality to reduce average memory access time.

terms.meaning: Interrupt-driven I/O is suitable for slow, character-oriented devices like printers that generate data/accept data infrequently.
terms.example: Printer interrupts CPU when it is ready to accept the next character.
terms.connects: Avoids CPU busy-waiting for slow peripheral devices.

terms.meaning: The condition code (flag) register is updated by the ALU to record the outcome of arithmetic/logic operations.
terms.example: ALU subtraction sets the carry flag if there is a borrow.
terms.connects: Flags from ALU operations are stored in the condition code register and used by conditional branch instructions.

given.plan: Match each concept to its primary associated hardware by reasoning about function: DMA for bulk devices (disk), Cache as fast RAM buffer, Interrupt I/O for slow devices (printer), CCR set by ALU.

steps.title: Match DMA I/O
steps.apply: DMA transfers large blocks of data efficiently for high-bandwidth storage devices. Hard disk (Disk, option 2) is the classic DMA device. So A->2.
steps.note: DMA is not used for cache (RAM) — that is a memory hierarchy issue, not I/O.

steps.title: Match Cache
steps.apply: Cache is physically built using high-speed SRAM (High speed RAM, option 1). So B->1.
steps.note: Cache sits between CPU and DRAM, implemented as SRAM.

steps.title: Match Interrupt I/O
steps.apply: Interrupt-driven I/O is used for slow devices. Printers (option 3) are classic interrupt-driven devices — they interrupt the CPU when ready for the next character. So C->3.
steps.note: Programmed I/O (polling) wastes CPU; interrupts let the CPU do other work until the slow device is ready.

steps.title: Match Condition Code Register
steps.apply: Condition code (flag) register stores the result flags produced by the ALU (option 4). So D->4.
steps.note: The CCR is tightly coupled to the ALU — flags like Zero, Carry, Overflow are set every time the ALU executes an operation.

solution.result: Correct matching: A-2 (DMA I/O -> Disk), B-1 (Cache -> High speed RAM), C-3 (Interrupt I/O -> Printer), D-4 (Condition Code Register -> ALU). Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Knowledge of I/O techniques: DMA for bulk/high-speed devices, interrupt-driven I/O for slow devices, cache as high-speed SRAM buffer, and condition code register as ALU output register.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements about synchronous and asynchronous I/O is NOT true?
A. An ISR is invoked on completion of I/O in synchronous I/O but not in asynchronous I/O
B. In both synchronous and asynchronous I/O, an ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) is invoked after completion of the I/O
C. A process makes a synchronous I/O call waits until I/O is complete, but a process making an asynchronous I/O call does not wait for completion of the I/O
D. In the case of asynchronous I/O, the process waiting for the completion of I/O is woken up by the ISR that is invoked after the completion of I/O
questions.answer: A. An ISR is invoked on completion of I/O in synchronous I/O but not in asynchronous I/O
questions.to_find: The statement that is NOT true about synchronous vs asynchronous I/O

understand.plain: In synchronous I/O, the calling process blocks and waits until the I/O operation is complete. In asynchronous I/O, the calling process continues execution and is notified (via an ISR/interrupt) when the I/O finishes. The key difference is whether the process waits or continues — but in BOTH cases the hardware completion triggers an interrupt/ISR at the OS level.

keywords.explain: The calling process blocks (waits) until the I/O operation completes before it can continue execution.
keywords.example: A read() system call that returns only after the data has been read from disk.

keywords.explain: The calling process initiates the I/O and immediately continues execution; it is notified when the I/O completes.
keywords.example: An aio_read() call that returns immediately; a signal or callback is delivered when the data is ready.

keywords.explain: A function invoked by the OS in response to a hardware interrupt, such as an I/O completion interrupt from a device controller.
keywords.example: When a disk DMA transfer completes, the disk controller raises an interrupt and the OS ISR handles it.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline comparison: Synchronous I/O shows process blocked during I/O, then ISR fires and unblocks the process. Asynchronous I/O shows process running during I/O, then ISR fires as notification callback.

given.aim: Identify the false statement among the four options about synchronous and asynchronous I/O.

terms.meaning: Claims ISR is invoked in synchronous I/O but NOT in asynchronous I/O.
terms.example: This is the opposite of what is commonly believed — actually ISR is invoked in both cases at the hardware level.
terms.connects: This is the NOT true statement: in both sync and async I/O, an interrupt/ISR is invoked at I/O completion.

terms.meaning: Claims ISR is invoked after I/O completion in both synchronous and asynchronous I/O.
terms.example: In synchronous I/O the ISR wakes the blocked process; in asynchronous I/O it delivers a notification.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — hardware always generates an interrupt on I/O completion regardless of sync/async.

terms.meaning: Claims a synchronous I/O caller waits for completion while an asynchronous I/O caller does not.
terms.example: sync: process blocks on read(); async: process calls aio_read() and continues running.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — it is the defining behavioral difference between the two I/O models.

terms.meaning: Claims in asynchronous I/O, the waiting process is woken up by the ISR after I/O completion.
terms.example: The OS ISR receives the I/O completion interrupt and signals or notifies the process that initiated the async I/O.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — the ISR is the mechanism that delivers the completion notification for async I/O.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against the true model of synchronous and asynchronous I/O. The incorrect statement (NOT true) is the one that contradicts the actual behavior.

steps.title: Analyze the role of ISR in both I/O modes
steps.apply: In any interrupt-driven I/O system (which includes both synchronous and asynchronous I/O in modern OS), the device controller raises a hardware interrupt when the I/O completes. The OS invokes the ISR to handle this interrupt in both cases.
steps.note: The difference is in what the ISR does: for synchronous I/O, it unblocks the waiting process; for asynchronous I/O, it delivers a completion notification or callback.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.apply: Statement A says 'ISR is invoked in synchronous I/O but NOT in asynchronous I/O.' This is FALSE. An ISR is invoked in BOTH synchronous and asynchronous I/O on I/O completion. In asynchronous I/O especially, the ISR is the critical mechanism that notifies the process of completion.
steps.note: If anything, the ISR is more visible/explicit in asynchronous I/O because the process is not waiting — it relies entirely on the ISR for the completion signal.

steps.title: Verify remaining statements are true
steps.apply: B is true (ISR invoked in both). C is true (defining difference: sync waits, async does not). D is true (ISR wakes the async waiting process). Only A contradicts reality.
steps.note: The question asks for NOT true, so the answer is A.

solution.result: Statement A is NOT true. An ISR is invoked on I/O completion in BOTH synchronous and asynchronous I/O, not only in synchronous I/O.

questions.formula_note: Synchronous I/O: caller blocks until completion (ISR unblocks it). Asynchronous I/O: caller continues execution; ISR delivers completion notification. ISR is used in both modes.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE with reason:
The flags are affected when conditional CALL or JUMP instructions are executed.
A. TRUE — conditional instructions always modify the flags register
B. FALSE — conditional CALL/JUMP instructions test the flags but do not modify them
C. TRUE — the program counter change causes flag updates
D. FALSE — only ALU instructions affect flags; CALL/JUMP instructions do not execute at all if the condition is false
questions.answer: B. FALSE — conditional CALL/JUMP instructions test the flags but do not modify them
questions.to_find: Whether conditional CALL or JUMP instructions affect (modify) the processor flags

understand.plain: Conditional CALL and conditional JUMP instructions check the current state of the processor flags (like zero flag, carry flag) to decide whether to branch. They READ the flags but do NOT WRITE or modify them. Only arithmetic and logic (ALU) instructions modify the flags. So the statement 'flags are affected' is FALSE.

keywords.explain: Single-bit registers in the status/condition code register that record properties of the last ALU result: Zero (Z), Carry (C), Sign (S), Overflow (V).
keywords.example: After SUB R1, R2: if result is 0, Z=1; if there was a borrow, C=1.

keywords.explain: An instruction that transfers control (changes PC) only if a specific flag condition is satisfied. It reads flags but does not set them.
keywords.example: JZ LABEL jumps to LABEL only if the Zero flag is 1; it does not alter any flags.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing: ALU instruction writes flags -> Condition code register. Conditional JUMP reads flags -> decision to branch or not. Arrow flows from ALU to CCR to JUMP (read only).

given.aim: Determine if conditional CALL or JUMP instructions modify the processor's flag/status register.

terms.meaning: Status bits set as a side effect of ALU operations.
terms.example: Z=1 after an ADD that produces result 0.
terms.connects: The question asks whether conditional branch instructions modify these flags.

terms.meaning: Tests a flag condition and changes the program counter (PC) if true, otherwise PC increments normally.
terms.example: JNZ LABEL: if Z=0, PC = LABEL address; else PC = PC+instruction_size.
terms.connects: The instruction uses flags as INPUT; the flags themselves are not changed.

terms.meaning: Like a conditional JUMP but also pushes the return address onto the stack before transferring control.
terms.example: CALL Z, SUBROUTINE: if Z=1, push PC+1 to stack, then PC = SUBROUTINE.
terms.connects: Same as JUMP regarding flags — reads but does not modify them.

given.plan: Recall the definition: flags are set by ALU instructions. Conditional branch instructions (JUMP, CALL) only read flags to make a decision; they never write to the flag register.

steps.title: Identify which instructions modify flags
steps.apply: In all standard ISAs (x86, 8085, etc.), only arithmetic and logical instructions (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, CMP, etc.) modify the flag/status register. Branch instructions like JUMP and CALL are control flow instructions that do NOT perform arithmetic.
steps.note: Some ISAs have special cases (e.g., x86 CALL pushes to stack but does not modify EFLAGS). The general rule stands: branch instructions read flags but do not write them.

steps.title: Apply to conditional CALL/JUMP
steps.apply: A conditional JUMP such as 'JZ addr' evaluates the Zero flag. If Z=1, PC is loaded with addr; if Z=0, PC is incremented. In neither case is the Zero flag (or any other flag) changed. Similarly for conditional CALL. Therefore the statement 'flags are affected' is FALSE.
steps.note: If flags were modified by branch instructions, it would break the programming model — subsequent conditional branches would read incorrect flag values.

solution.result: FALSE. Conditional CALL or JUMP instructions test the flags to decide whether to branch but do NOT modify the flags. Only ALU instructions affect the processor flags.

questions.formula_note: Control flow instructions (JUMP, CALL, RET) read the condition code register but never write to it. The CCR is updated exclusively by arithmetic/logic (ALU) instructions.

questions.question: Many of the advanced microprocessors prefetch instructions and store them in an instruction buffer to speed up processing. This speed up is achieved because ________
questions.answer: The CPU can overlap instruction fetch and instruction execution, hiding the memory access latency by fetching the next instruction(s) while the current instruction is being executed.
questions.to_find: The reason why prefetching instructions into an instruction buffer speeds up processor execution

understand.plain: Without prefetching, the CPU must wait for memory to deliver each instruction before it can execute it — this wastes time on memory latency. With an instruction buffer (prefetch queue), the CPU fetches upcoming instructions into the buffer during idle cycles (e.g., while the current instruction executes). This overlapping of fetch and execute hides memory latency and keeps the execution unit busy.

keywords.explain: Fetching instructions from memory before they are needed for execution, and storing them in a buffer (prefetch queue) so the CPU does not stall waiting for memory.
keywords.example: While the CPU executes instruction I, the memory bus is used to fetch instruction I+1 into the prefetch buffer.

keywords.explain: A small FIFO buffer between the instruction fetch unit and the decode/execute unit that holds prefetched instructions.
keywords.example: Intel 8086 had a 6-byte prefetch queue; while the BIU fetched instructions, the EU decoded and executed them.

keywords.explain: Hiding the delay of memory access by doing the fetch in parallel with execution of the prior instruction.
keywords.example: If memory takes 5 cycles and execution takes 5 cycles, pipelining fetch+execute gives 5 cycles per instruction instead of 10.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline diagram showing two rows: 'Without Prefetch' where Fetch I1, Exec I1, Fetch I2, Exec I2 happen sequentially. 'With Prefetch' where Exec and next Fetch overlap, doubling throughput.

given.aim: Explain why prefetching into an instruction buffer speeds up microprocessor execution.

terms.meaning: Without prefetch, the CPU alternates between fetching (reading from memory) and executing, so the execution unit is idle during fetch and the memory bus is idle during execute.
terms.example: For a 10-ns memory and 10-ns execution, each instruction takes 20 ns without prefetch.
terms.connects: This is the inefficiency that prefetching eliminates.

terms.meaning: With an instruction buffer, the CPU can fetch the next instruction(s) from memory while simultaneously executing the current instruction.
terms.example: While executing I1 (10 ns), the BIU fetches I2 into the buffer (10 ns) — effective throughput is one instruction per 10 ns.
terms.connects: This is the core reason for the speedup.

terms.meaning: A FIFO holding prefetched instructions so the decode unit always has the next instruction ready without waiting for memory.
terms.example: Intel 8086's 6-byte prefetch queue allows the BIU to keep ahead of the EU.
terms.connects: The buffer decouples the fetch rate from the execute rate, tolerating memory latency spikes.

given.plan: Identify the cause of slowdown (sequential fetch and execute), then show how prefetching overlaps these phases, effectively hiding memory access latency and improving throughput.

steps.title: Identify the bottleneck without prefetching
steps.apply: Without prefetching, instruction fetch from memory and instruction execution are purely sequential: the CPU fetches an instruction, then executes it, then fetches the next, etc. During fetch, the execution unit is idle. During execution, the memory bus is idle. This sequential model wastes half the available resources.
steps.note: Memory access is typically slower than internal CPU execution, so the fetch step is often the bottleneck.

steps.title: Explain how prefetch buffer enables overlap
steps.formula_raw: Effective_CPI = max(Fetch_cycles, Execute_cycles) instead of Fetch_cycles + Execute_cycles
steps.apply: With an instruction prefetch buffer: while the CPU is executing instruction I, the memory interface prefetches instruction I+1 (and possibly I+2, I+3...) into the buffer. When execution of I completes, I+1 is immediately available in the buffer — no memory wait. The fetch and execute phases now overlap (pipeline), reducing the effective time per instruction.
steps.note: This is a simple 2-stage pipeline (Fetch || Execute). It works well for sequential instruction streams; branches can partially invalidate the buffer (the prefetched instructions after a taken branch are discarded).

solution.result: The speedup is achieved because the CPU can overlap instruction fetch (from memory into the buffer) with instruction execution. While the current instruction executes, the next instruction is already being fetched. This hides memory access latency and keeps the execution unit continuously busy, increasing throughput.

questions.formula_note: Instruction prefetch implements a simple fetch-execute pipeline (2 stages). Speedup comes from overlapping memory access latency with computation, so effective instruction throughput approaches max(fetch_time, execute_time) rather than their sum.
